summary: Stancil argues that Paul Graham’s widely cited math on a startup wealth tax is not wrong so much as misleading, because it relies on extreme, compounding assumptions about growth rates, time horizons, and equity outcomes. Small tweaks to those assumptions swing the results wildly, revealing that spreadsheet models in this context mostly reflect prior policy beliefs rather than hard truths.
money_quotes: Exponential growth is extraordinarily sensitive to seemingly insignificant changes.
With such a range of possibilities available, anyone with an Excel spreadsheet can easily craft a story to fit their narrative.
These simple models are just a Rorschach test for pre-existing policy preferences.
By Graham’s own math, even with Warren’s wealth tax, a founder who puts in six years of work—the six years they earn above-market growth rates—will be worth more than $100 million for 230 years.
key_insight: Wealth-tax impact estimates for startup founders hinge on extreme exponential-growth assumptions, so the headline numbers say more about the modeler’s priors than about the policy itself.
